speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 24, 1996. The subject of the interview, Mr. Drexel Sprecher. The place of the interview, Chevy Chase, Maryland, United States. The interviewer is Judge Bruce Einhorn. The language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: One more time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 24, 1996. The subject of the interview is Mr. Drexel Sprecher. The interviewer is Judge Bruce Einhorn. The place of the interview is Chevy Chase, Maryland in the United States. The language of the interview is and will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, good afternoon. Would you please state your name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Drexel A. Sprecher-- D-R-E-X-E-L, A, S-P-R-E-C-H-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do you now reside, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Elizabeth Condominium, apartment 608, 4601 North Park Avenue, Chevy Chase, Maryland 20815.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Sprecher, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Independence, Wisconsin on March 25, 1913.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walter E. Sprecher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when and where he was born, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1913? Somewhere I think about 1885, something like that. I don't remember the exact date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember whereabouts?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Independence, Wisconsin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his occupation in Wisconsin?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a banker--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and, and an investor in real estate and farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your father pass away?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Mayo Clinic in Rochester, Minnesota.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, sir. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Florence [? Laverne ?] Malloy. She was of Irish extraction and came from Winona, Minnesota before she married my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the year of her birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it'd be about two years younger than my father. And she died in 1915 in an automobile accident.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she work outside the home at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did you continue to live in Independence, Wisconsin after you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] up until I graduated from the, from Harvard Law School in 1938, except for the times that I was away at college and when I was traveling abroad or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like growing up in Independence?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like growing up in Independence, Wisconsin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a typical small town life of that time. Everybody knew everybody else. There was a lot of Polish people in that particular town and quite a few Norwegians. So there was quite a mix, ethnically.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if I may ask, what was your family's religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was evangelical, evangelical, later turned to Methodist. The Methodists absorbed that particular group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a particularly religious household?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were. I, I would say my father was very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak any other languages at home other than English?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of your high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Independence High School.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you graduate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I graduated there in 1926. Wait a minute, in 1930. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a boy growing into young adulthood, did you have any particular career goals, any ambitions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose at first I kind of wanted to follow in my father's footsteps. But after I got off to the university and the college, I acquired a different interests. And at first I wanted to become a professor.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to college, where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I first went one year to a small Christian college, North Central College at Naperville, Illinois. But in my sophomore year, I went to the University of Wisconsin and continued there until I graduated in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what campus did you attend at University of Wisconsin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Letters and Science, the main campus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you graduate?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of a degree did you receive?

speaker: interviewee
text: BS, Bachelor of Science.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you concentrate in a particular field or study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Economics and political science, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a large amount of history as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was college like?

speaker: interviewee
text: College was very busy for me. I became involved immediately in a lot of athletic as well as campus activities that didn't have anything to do with-- nothing directly to do with my studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of athletic activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was first on a basketball team at the college, the freshman basketball team. And then-- and also on the second team of the football, actually. Then I went to Wisconsin. I was on the basketball, the second basketball team. And later I was on the Wisconsin crew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about social activities? You mentioned you were involved with some.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of the, the initiation into friendship with a couple of liberal pastors, Reverend Shorty Collins, Dr. Alfred W. Swan, who was a congregationalist-- neither of these persons had the same religious background as I did. But both of them had a great influence on me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was introduced to some other students who were liberal, and among other things there was quite a bit of prejudice on the campus by some of the Midwesterners. And some of us decided we wanted to do something about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a group of us who were pretty active on the campus formed a group called Koinos, K-O-I-N-O-S, which is a Greek word for 'in common.' And we didn't admit anybody to this group unless he was pretty well known on campus and unless he had a B average, he or she had a B average. And as a result, we got quite a bit of attention, and we held meetings every three weeks or so, a luncheon meeting. And this became a kind of a visible thing on, on campus. Were you involved in any other activities of note in college?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a member of a, a fraternity, Phi Gamma Delta. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was kind of known as the special liberal in that house, in that fraternity house, most of whom were quite wealthy, including Fred Maytag, the maker of the washing machines, and Bill Vilter, the maker of the machines which are used to cool, were used to cool theaters 40 years ago, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend further schooling after University of Wisconsin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I immediately went to the University of London and attended the London School of Economics and Political Science for one year. But I was not seeking a degree, so I spent about a third of my time traveling on the continent of Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1934, beginning in September and lasting until about August of the next year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I traveled throughout Europe, throughout the northern part of Europe, to Holland and France, Nazi Germany-- I spent over three or four weeks in Nazi Germany altogether-- Switzerland, Poland, Soviet Russia, Sweden, where I had an interview with the prime minister of Sweden when I was 22 years old, Norway, Scotland, Great Britain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why did you have an interview with the prime minister of Sweden at age 22?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Hyde Park early in my years at the university, at the London School of Economics, to attend, not as a participant, but as a boor, an onlooker, a meeting that was being held by Sir Oswald Mosley, M-O-S-L-E-Y, who was the head of the British fascists. And so I was with this group of students and other British people, mainly, who were booing. And we were separated from this demonstration, this meeting which they were having in Hyde Park, by police. The fascists needed protection [LAUGHS] by the police because there were so many people opposed over there. At that meeting I met a fellow by the name of Fred Heydey, whose father was a Labour member of parliament. He immediately asked me up to Nottingham to visit his family. And his father was a high official in the British trade union movement. And I became fairly friendly with that family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when Heydey's father heard that I was going to make a trip to the continent he said, are you going to Sweden? And I said, yes. He says, one of my fellow members of the International Labor Organization is a, is the main trade union leader in Sweden. He said, would you like to meet him? And I said, of course. So he gave me a letter. When I got to this trade union leader in Sweden, I started to ask him all kinds of political questions including the relationships between Sweden and Nazi Germany. He says, you know, I'm not the man to answer these questions. You should ask these questions of the prime minister. I said, but I don't have a letter to the prime minister. He said, well, we'll correct that. So he then wrote out a little scribble, and with this note I went to the prime minister's office. The executive administrative assistant to the prime minister spoke English very well. And he had me write out a bunch of questions. And these were answered in, in the handwriting of the prime minister of Sweden first, and then I had an interview with him, which was quite a thing to have arranged for a young man of 22. I can show you the results in the other room, because I made a report on it to my local paper in, in the Midwest. Why did you go to Germany during this tour of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, partly because I wanted to see what was happening in Germany beyond what I already knew from courses I took in London at the London School. There were many refugees from Hitler already that we had come across, and including a couple of members of the faculty. So I thought as a good Nordic, it would be a good thing to, [LAUGHS] to get a little experience in what life was really like in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through a friend of mine, who was attending Oxford, we had arranged to go to the home of someone in Dusseldorf, a woman whose husband was a member of the SA, the storm troops. He was not there at the time. But she entertained, or put in residence, students from England. And instead of a student from England she got one from America, in my case.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed there for a week or 10 days and traveled up to Oberhausen to the factories of one of the main [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] iron and steel works and to the Krupp works, and was shown through and given quite a bit of high-level treatment, because they somehow assumed that with my Nordic looks and all, I might be more sympathetic to them. And, in fact, I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak German at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] I can speak some German, but not very well. I had three and a half years in college, but that didn't help very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what were your impressions, Mr. Sprecher?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your impressions of Germany in 1934?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I spent some time talking to Germans. I went out to taverns or beer halls with them. And I tried to explore what their thinking was and so forth. And one evening, I remember a, a gentleman I should mention, by the name of Stuart Herman, who was a student at Strasbourg University, a theological school. He and I had gone down to Freiburg. And we've noticed that one of the young women in the group that was talking to us, maybe four or five people that had gathered around a table, was very quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I arranged, under some pretext or other, to get her off to the side. And I said, you seem to be very quiet. I suspect that you don't want to talk too much in front of these other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, that's right, and I'm an anti-Nazi, and I always will be. But I have to worry about being thrown in jail. So that was one kind of experience. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Nazis-- the Germans had a couple of students studying in London. And one of them introduced me to his father, who was a director of the [GERMAN] which is the third largest iron  and steel company. And I traveled with her. We both went to Berlin. We didn't travel together. And I met her there. And we went to the famous film, [GERMAN], which was a production by Reni, R-E-N-I, Riefenstahl, a famous woman German producer, who produced this film which highly pleased Hitler and the Nazis. And we saw this film, and there would be applause during the part of it, and so on. But obviously, that was just part of the propaganda putting on to get control of the minds of ordinary Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak to any German Jews while in Germany at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I spoke to a British [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] newsman, a journalist, who was Jewish. And he was there kind of on tenterhooks, because the Nazis obviously didn't like it, but they didn't want to be in a position of excluding him, because of his connections, I think, to the But I did not speak to any Jewish people at that time in Germany. I spoke to many of them later in Poland, but that's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were your conclusions about the Germany of 1934 as you headed back to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had made my conclusions up long before I got there. When I was at the University of Wisconsin, I wrote a long paper on which I got an A-plus-plus, called 'The Structure of National Socialism.' And I submitted this to one of my professors. And he thought it was quite outstanding. As a matter of fact, that was one reason I had received a fellowship to the University of Chicago. But I turned that fellowship down in order to go to London, because several professors told me you ought to get out of the country for a year and study. And so I did. And since I could afford it, I went to study on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those conclusions in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conclusions of your report, the ones that didn't change after you went to Germany? Could you summarize them for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, there was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a regime of oppression, where people were thrown into concentration camps. And that was known in America among scholarly circles and among many other people, for that matter, and that there was a persecution of the Jews. The boycott of the Jews in 1933, in April, was well known, the German boycott, the Nazi boycott.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to England already convinced that the Nazis were a very evil force and that I would always be opposed to them. And nothing I learned in London, I can assure you, changed my mind. When you went back stateside, what did you do after the University of London?

speaker: interviewee
text: I entered Harvard Law School, and did the usual kinds of things, except that I became president of the Harvard Law School Union and spent a good deal of my time on arranging for evening seminars that were not put on by the school, but which they allowed me to put on, and getting in some liberal speakers. Whenever speakers came to the Ford Hall Forum in Boston, I'd try to get them to come out and speak at Harvard Law School.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an idea, if you would, of some of the speakers you had or the kinds of speakers you had.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Governor Phil La Follette of the state of Wisconsin, whom I had not met before, but he came to speak at the Ford Hall Forum. The head of the-- the director of the Ford Hall Forum, David Niles, suggested I use my car to pick up the governor at the airport and bring him in and that I, I'd make a good contact in Wisconsin. So I did. I went out and picked up the governor and brought him into Boston and got to know him pretty well. He went to visit the governor of the state of Massachusetts, who was a fellow by the name of Curley. And he asked me to go along. So I got to know the governor of the state of Massachusetts as well. It's just one of these things where, if you, you play it well and if you're fairly loquacious, you get asked along, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you politically active at Harvard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as head of the Harvard Law School Union, I, I think we had a good third of the students join it. But we had no particular requirements as to what your politics were. But I would say that most of those students were on the liberal side and very pro-New Deal. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This is at the time of the-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] can you cut this off? You want me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we can go on. I-- did you like law school? What were your impressions of law school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say that again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like law school? What were your impressions of Harvard Law School?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Harvard Law School was a, a very great discipline. And I nearly didn't survive the first year. But after that, I learned how to beat the system, and I got good notebooks from other, from other students who were a year ahead of me, and-- because I hated going to the long classes. So I only attended about half the classes in Harvard. And that's how it went. I think I better answer that. It may be something--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Did you like law school? Did you enjoy it once you learned that system?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't particularly enjoy it. And I was very glad-- actually, my-- I got involved in the moot court arguments. And I was in the Powell Club. And professor Thomas Reed Powell was the person we honored. And we would, now and then, have meetings with him, with beer and without. He was very friendly to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That club won the Ames Competition that year, which was the moot court arguments. And it happened to involve a case which was about to be decided by the Supreme Court.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forget the name of it now, but I can give you a copy or show you a copy in the next room. Because it's all bound up because of the fact that we won, and so on. It involved a labor case which was before the United States Supreme Court. And our briefs were actually used by the Department of Justice later in part of their preparations. Because we had spent a great deal of time and developed materials that they thought were helpful to them. And I took those briefs and came down to Washington after I graduated from Harvard Law School. And I wanted one of two jobs at the time. One was as an attorney of the National Labor Relations Board, because I was very interested in strengthening the unions in the country. And the other was antitrust division of the Department of Justice. And the reason was because I felt there was too much big business and monopoly in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you graduate Harvard?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you graduate Harvard Law School?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938. In the fall of '38, I took this brief I had mentioned, and I went down, and I went into the office of the general counsel, Charles Fay of the National Labor Relations Board. And he and his associate, a man by the name of Watts, hired me within one half hour. So I never got to the Department of Justice to make an application there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin on the legal staff of the National Labor Relations Board?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September of 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay with that organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with them until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] early May 1942, but I was moved around the country a lot because I very quickly was put into the trial section. And I was given, after a little breaking in, and often without enough breaking in, the assignment to try a number of cases in San Diego and Chicago and the backwoods of West Virginia and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the subject matter of the cases you tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfair labor practices underneath the National Labor Relations Act, which had been passed in 1935, and which had been found constitutional by the Supreme Court after a long fight. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, that's pretty much it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find the work? How did you find the work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found it very interesting. I can remember my first trial, which was in San Diego with the Star and Crescent Boat Company, which had been engaged in firing union employees and in trying to form a little company union. And I was so intrigued, and I also felt so overburdened that I didn't sleep the first two nights of the trial, and I practically fell asleep on the third night of the-- or the third day of the trial. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you living in the DC area when you were working out of the National Labor Relations Board?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of the time. But for instance, I was assigned to California for about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two months. And then I became involved out there in a very ticklish situation, which involved the associated farmers who had committed assaults on some picketers outside the JG Boswell Company, which was one of the largest producers of economic wares in products in California at that time. And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went down to hold some meetings, and the associated farmers stationed cars across from my little hotel out at this small town where this, some of this violence had taken part. And the associated farmers are trying to intimidate any witnesses from coming in the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the minister, who was the, actually the local organizer of these workers, came in and said, you aren't going to have any more witnesses, because those two cars are scaring them away. So I hired a newspaper boy to go over and find the names out, if he could find the names of a couple of them. He did, and I phoned them into Los Angeles. I said, I'm going over there, and I just want you to know the number of these two cars that are intimidating me and all that, and I'm going over to open the car doors. So I went over there, and I opened the door, and I said, I want to read you Section 12 of the National Labor Relations Act and to let you know that you're interfering with a federal officer in the performance of his duties. And each of you is likely to go to jail and be fined up to-- and I read them what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: This scared the hell out of them. And they backed up and went away. And on the other side of the street, some people clapped, because they knew that these people were trying to intimidate me, and they didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: On that note, I think we're going to change tapes. Thank you. Give us a moment.
